title: Rotorua

infobox.value: (Māori pronunciation: [[ɾɔtɔˈɾʉa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Rotorua_Pronunciation.wav "File:Rotorua Pronunciation.wav")[[8]](#cite_note-8)) is a city in the [Bay of Plenty region](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_region "Bay of Plenty region") of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand")'s [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island"). It is sited on the southern shores of [Lake Rotorua](/wiki/Lake_Rotorua "Lake Rotorua"), from which it takes its name. It is the seat of the [Rotorua Lakes District](/wiki/Rotorua_Lakes_District "Rotorua Lakes District"), a [territorial authority](/wiki/Territorial_authorities_of_New_Zealand "Territorial authorities of New Zealand") encompassing Rotorua and several other nearby towns. It has an estimated resident population of 58,500, making it the country's [13th largest urban area](/wiki/List_of_New_Zealand_urban_areas_by_population "List of New Zealand urban areas by population"), and the Bay of Plenty's second-largest urban area behind [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga").

**Rotorua** (Māori pronunciation: [[ɾɔtɔˈɾʉa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Rotorua_Pronunciation.wav "File:Rotorua Pronunciation.wav")[[8]](#cite_note-8)) is a city in the [Bay of Plenty region](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_region "Bay of Plenty region") of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand")'s [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island"). It is sited on the southern shores of [Lake Rotorua](/wiki/Lake_Rotorua "Lake Rotorua"), from which it takes its name. It is the seat of the [Rotorua Lakes District](/wiki/Rotorua_Lakes_District "Rotorua Lakes District"), a [territorial authority](/wiki/Territorial_authorities_of_New_Zealand "Territorial authorities of New Zealand") encompassing Rotorua and several other nearby towns. It has an estimated resident population of 58,500, making it the country's [13th largest urban area](/wiki/List_of_New_Zealand_urban_areas_by_population "List of New Zealand urban areas by population"), and the Bay of Plenty's second-largest urban area behind [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga").

[Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa") Māori first settled in Rotorua in the 14th century, and a thriving [pā](/wiki/P%C4%81 "Pā") was established at [Ohinemutu](/wiki/Ohinemutu "Ohinemutu") by the people who would become [Ngāti Whakaue](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Whakaue "Ngāti Whakaue"). The city became closely associated with conflict during the [Musket Wars](/wiki/Musket_Wars "Musket Wars") of the 1820s. Ohinemutu was invaded by a [Ngāpuhi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81puhi "Ngāpuhi")-led coalition in 1823, commanded by [Hongi Hika](/wiki/Hongi_Hika "Hongi Hika") and [Pōmare I](/wiki/P%C5%8Dmare_I_(Ng%C4%81puhi) "Pōmare I (Ngāpuhi)"). In the 19th century early European settlers had an interest in developing Rotorua, due to its unique [geothermal](/wiki/Geothermal_gradient "Geothermal gradient") activity in Rotorua and its surrounding area.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Then, efforts by Māori and Europeans alike to establish Rotorua as a [spa town](/wiki/Spa_town "Spa town") led to a [99-year lease](/wiki/99-year_lease "99-year lease") of land from Ngāti Whakaue to the Government. The city first became a major site of tourism due to the Rotorua's close proximity to the [Pink and White Terraces](/wiki/Pink_and_White_Terraces "Pink and White Terraces"), until they were destroyed by the [volcanic eruption](/wiki/1886_eruption_of_Mount_Tarawera "1886 eruption of Mount Tarawera") of [Mount Tarawera](/wiki/Mount_Tarawera "Mount Tarawera") in 1886. Rotorua was elevated to borough status in 1922 and to city status 40 years later.

Rotorua is a major destination for both domestic and international tourists. It is known for its geothermal activity and Māori cultural tourism, and features [geysers](/wiki/Geyser "Geyser") – notably the [Pōhutu Geyser](/wiki/P%C5%8Dhutu_Geyser "Pōhutu Geyser") at [Whakarewarewa](/wiki/Whakarewarewa "Whakarewarewa") – and hot mud pools. This thermal activity is sourced to the [Rotorua Caldera](/wiki/Rotorua_Caldera "Rotorua Caldera"), in which the city lies.

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

The name Rotorua comes from the [Māori language](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"), where the full name for the city and lake is *Te Rotorua-nui-a-Kahumatamomoe*.[[10]](#cite_note-10) *Roto* means 'lake' and *rua* means 'two' or in this case, 'second' – Rotorua thus meaning 'Second lake'. [Kahumatamomoe](/wiki/Kahumatamomoe "Kahumatamomoe") was the uncle of the [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") chief [Ihenga](/wiki/Ihenga "Ihenga"), the ancestral explorer of the [Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa").[[11]](#cite_note-WisesRotorua-11) It was the second major lake the chief discovered, and he dedicated it to his uncle. It is the largest of a multitude of lakes found to the northeast, all connected with the Rotorua Caldera and nearby [Mount Tarawera](/wiki/Mount_Tarawera "Mount Tarawera"). The name can also mean the equally appropriate 'Crater lake'.[[11]](#cite_note-WisesRotorua-11)

A common nickname for Rotorua is "Sulphur City" due to the [hydrogen sulphide](/wiki/Hydrogen_sulphide "Hydrogen sulphide") emissions, which gives the city a smell similar to "rotten eggs",[[1]](#cite_note-NZHerald3511691-1) as well as "Rotten-rua" combining its legitimate name and the rotten smell prevalent.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Another common nickname is "Roto-Vegas", likening the city's own strip of road flanked by businesses and restaurants to [that of Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Strip "Las Vegas Strip").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The area was initially settled by [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") of the [Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa") [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") in the 14th century, and a thriving [pā](/wiki/P%C4%81 "Pā") was built at [Ohinemutu](/wiki/Ohinemutu "Ohinemutu") by the shores of [Lake Rotorua](/wiki/Lake_Rotorua "Lake Rotorua"). According to Te Arawa folklore, the city's bountiful [geothermal springs](/wiki/Hot_spring "Hot spring") had resulted from a plea by [Ngātoro-i-rangi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81toro-i-rangi "Ngātoro-i-rangi"), an ancestral [tohunga](/wiki/Tohunga "Tohunga"), for the gods to send fire-bearing spirits from [Hawaiki](/wiki/Hawaiki "Hawaiki"), the semi-mythological Māori homeland. The Te Arawa Māori who lived at Ohinemutu eventually began to call themselves [Ngāti Whakaue](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Whakaue "Ngāti Whakaue"), after their ancestor [Whakaue Kaipapa](/wiki/Maketu "Maketu").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Rotorua was a site of heavy conflict during the [Musket Wars](/wiki/Musket_Wars "Musket Wars"). During the early 1820s, the large Northern [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") [Ngāpuhi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81puhi "Ngāpuhi") had begun expanding outwards further south, driving [Ngāti Pāoa](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_P%C4%81oa "Ngāti Pāoa") and their chief [Te Hīnaki](/w/index.php?title=Te_H%C4%ABnaki&action=edit&redlink=1 "Te Hīnaki (page does not exist)") from modern-day [South Auckland](/wiki/South_Auckland "South Auckland"), and launched periodic raids into the [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_region "Bay of Plenty region").[[15]](#cite_note-:4-15) A military expedition into the Bay of Plenty by a combined Ngāpuhi and [Ngāti Maru nō Hauraki](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Maru_(Hauraki) "Ngāti Maru (Hauraki)") force in 1818 had set the stage for further raids in the modern Rotorua area. Ngāpuhi and its allies launched an ambitious invasion of the [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato") in 1822, with a battalion led by junior chief [Te Pae-o-te-Rangi](/wiki/Te_Wera_Hauraki#Mokoia_campaign,_1823 "Te Wera Hauraki") were ambushed and slaughtered by [Ngāti Whakaue](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Whakaue "Ngāti Whakaue") for trespassing into Rotorua. This was apparently at the insistence of [Te Rauparaha](/wiki/Te_Rauparaha "Te Rauparaha"); under Māori customary law the attack demanded *[utu](/wiki/Utu_(M%C4%81ori_concept) "Utu (Māori concept)")*. Ngāpuhi commander [Hongi Hika](/wiki/Hongi_Hika "Hongi Hika") convened with his fellow chiefs [Pōmare I](/wiki/P%C5%8Dmare_I_(Ng%C4%81puhi) "Pōmare I (Ngāpuhi)") and [Te Wera Hauraki](/wiki/Te_Wera_Hauraki "Te Wera Hauraki") to propose war, and thus in February 1823 a Ngāpuhi-led coalition invaded Rotorua.[[16]](#cite_note-16): 242 The force, which also included [Ngāti Whātua](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Wh%C4%81tua "Ngāti Whātua") and some [Waikato Tainui](/wiki/Waikato_Tainui "Waikato Tainui"), landed at [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga") and headed up the [Pongakawa](/wiki/Pongakawa "Pongakawa") valley to attack [Mokoia Island](/wiki/Mokoia_Island "Mokoia Island")[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETarakawa1899242-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) Te Arawa fell back after the loss of 170 men and were defeated by Ngāpuhi,[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETarakawa1899244-245-20) and utu was satisfied.[[15]](#cite_note-:4-15)

The first European in the area was probably [Phillip Tapsell](/wiki/Phillip_Tapsell "Phillip Tapsell") who was trading from the [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty "Bay of Plenty") coast at [Maketu](/wiki/Maketu "Maketu") from 1828. He later married into Te Arawa and became highly regarded by them.[[21]](#cite_note-NZETapsell-21) Missionaries [Henry Williams](/wiki/Henry_Williams_(missionary) "Henry Williams (missionary)") and Thomas Chapman visited in 1831[[11]](#cite_note-WisesRotorua-11) and Chapman and his wife established a mission at Te Koutu in 1835.[[22]](#cite_note-NZDBChapman-22) This was abandoned within a year, but Chapman returned in 1838 and established a second mission at Mokoia Island.[[11]](#cite_note-WisesRotorua-11)[[22]](#cite_note-NZDBChapman-22)

The lakeshore was a prominent site of skirmishes during the [New Zealand Wars](/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars "New Zealand Wars") of the 1860s. [William Fox](/wiki/William_Fox_(New_Zealand_politician) "William Fox (New Zealand politician)") advocated for turning the Rotorua region into a [national park](/wiki/National_park "National park"), inspired by the [Yellowstone](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23) Conversely, the Te Arawa community suggested the establishment of a township centred around Rotorua's thermal springs, with the intent of developing a [Polynesian Spa](/wiki/Polynesian_Spa "Polynesian Spa") and [health resort](/wiki/Destination_spa "Destination spa") where tourists could indulge in hot pools.[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23) In 1880, instead of selling the land, the [Ngāti Whakaue](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Whakaue "Ngāti Whakaue") people leased 50 acres to the Crown under the [Fenton](/wiki/Francis_Dart_Fenton "Francis Dart Fenton") Agreement,[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) granting the government the authority to offer 99-year leases on their behalf.[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23) Revenues from leases helped fund [Rotorua Boys' High School](/wiki/Rotorua_Boys%27_High_School "Rotorua Boys' High School"), and increased significantly upon the expiry of the 99-year leases.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The eponymous Fenton Street in the modern city's CBD bears the judge's name.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Nevertheless, the enactment of the *Thermal Springs District Act* in 1881 gave the government exclusive rights to both purchase and lease lands containing hot springs, lakes, or river, and as a result, by the turn of the century, nearly half of the Rotorua blocks were sold.[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23) In 1993, the Crown settled a [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") claim with the Ngāti Whakaue people to honour their broken contract, by agreeing to return the gifted lands that were no longer required for their original use.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The town was connected to Auckland with the opening of the [Rotorua Branch](/wiki/Rotorua_Branch "Rotorua Branch") railway and commencement of the [Rotorua Express](/wiki/Rotorua_Express "Rotorua Express") train in 1894, resulting in the rapid growth of the town and tourism from this time forward. Guidebooks about the 'Land of Boiling Water' also proliferated.[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23) During the 1880s, tourists, especially from Australia, started visiting Rotorua to witness its natural marvels like the [Pink and White Terraces](/wiki/Pink_and_White_Terraces "Pink and White Terraces") until these were destroyed in a [volcanic eruption in 1886](/wiki/1886_eruption_of_Mount_Tarawera "1886 eruption of Mount Tarawera").[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23) Rotorua was established as a borough in 1922, elected its first [mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_Rotorua "Mayor of Rotorua") in 1923, and declared a city in 1962 before becoming a [district](/wiki/Rotorua_Lakes "Rotorua Lakes") in 1979.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

### Setting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Setting")]

Main article: [Rotorua Caldera](/wiki/Rotorua_Caldera "Rotorua Caldera")

The city of Rotorua and the adjacent Lake Rotorua are located within the Rotorua Caldera that was formed in a major volcanic eruption approximately 240,000 years ago. The caldera is the source of the geothermal activity that is a key feature of the city and surrounding region.[[30]](#cite_note-Gravley2007-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Climate")]

The Rotorua region enjoys a mild [temperate climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") ([Cfb](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")). Rotorua is situated inland from the coast and is sheltered by high country to the south and east of the city, resulting in less wind than many other places in New Zealand. During the winter months, June – August, temperatures can drop below 0 °C. Frost is common in Rotorua during its winter months, with an average of 57 ground frosts annually, and 20 nights per year below 0 °C. Snowfall in Rotorua is rare, and since the 1970s has only been recorded twice. On 15 August 2011 and 13 July 2017, snowflakes fell in the town centre, and during the July 2017 snowfall, snow accumulated in the nearby Mamaku ranges and in the outer reaches of the district, where snowfall occurs on average once every three years.

### Lakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Lakes")]

Main article: [Lakes of Rotorua](/wiki/Lakes_of_Rotorua "Lakes of Rotorua")

The Rotorua region has 17 lakes, known collectively as the Lakes of Rotorua. Fishing, waterskiing, swimming and other water activities are popular in summer. Several of the lakes are stocked for sports fishing with trout from the [Fish and Game New Zealand](/wiki/Fish_and_Game_New_Zealand "Fish and Game New Zealand") hatchery at [Ngongotahā](/wiki/Ngongotah%C4%81 "Ngongotahā"). The lakes are also used for event venues; Rotorua hosted the 2007 World Waterski Championships and Lake Rotorua was the venue for the World Blind Sailing Championships in March 2009.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Lake Rotorua is also used as a departure and landing point for float planes.

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Demography")]

The Rotorua urban area, as defined by Statistics New Zealand, covers 46.06 km2 (17.78 sq mi) and incorporates 29 statistical areas.[[37]](#cite_note-Area-37) It has an estimated population of 58,500 as of June 2025.[[7]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-7)

Rotorua had a population of 55,326 in the [2023 New Zealand census](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_census "2023 New Zealand census"), an increase of 1,140 people (2.1%) since the [2018 census](/wiki/2018_New_Zealand_census "2018 New Zealand census"), and an increase of 6,429 people (13.1%) since the [2013 census](/wiki/2013_New_Zealand_census "2013 New Zealand census"). There were 26,883 males, 28,254 females, and 189 people of [other genders](/wiki/Non-binary_gender "Non-binary gender") in 19,137 dwellings.[[39]](#cite_note-39) 2.7% of people identified as [LGBTIQ+](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ"). The median age was 35.6 years (compared with 38.1 years nationally). There were 12,270 people (22.2%) aged under 15 years, 10,878 (19.7%) aged 15 to 29, 23,739 (42.9%) aged 30 to 64, and 8,442 (15.3%) aged 65 or older.[[38]](#cite_note-Census_2023-38)

### Ethnic group

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ethnic group")]

People could identify as more than one ethnicity. The results were 57.1% [European](/wiki/European_New_Zealanders "European New Zealanders") ([Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā")); 46.0% [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"); 7.3% [Pasifika](/wiki/Pasifika_New_Zealanders "Pasifika New Zealanders"); 11.9% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_New_Zealanders "Asian New Zealanders"); 0.7% Middle Eastern, Latin American and African New Zealanders (MELAA); and 1.9% other, which includes people giving their ethnicity as "New Zealander".

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Languages")]

English was spoken by 95.6%, Māori by 14.7%, Samoan by 0.5%, and other languages by 10.9%. No language could be spoken by 2.3% (e.g. too young to talk). [New Zealand Sign Language](/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language "New Zealand Sign Language") was known by 0.6%. The percentage of people born overseas was 19.0, compared with 28.8% nationally.[[38]](#cite_note-Census_2023-38)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Religion")]

Religious affiliations were 31.9% [Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_New_Zealand "Christianity in New Zealand"), 2.1% [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism_in_New_Zealand "Hinduism in New Zealand"), 0.4% [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_New_Zealand "Islam in New Zealand"), 3.9% [Māori religious beliefs](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_religious_beliefs "Māori religious beliefs"), 0.8% [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism_in_New_Zealand "Buddhism in New Zealand"), 0.4% [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age"), 0.1% [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism_in_New_Zealand "Judaism in New Zealand"), and 1.9% other religions. People who answered that they had [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_New_Zealand "Irreligion in New Zealand") were 51.6%, and 7.2% of people did not answer the census question.[[38]](#cite_note-Census_2023-38)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Education")]

Of those at least 15 years old, 8,322 (19.3%) people had a bachelor's or higher degree, 23,331 (54.2%) had a post-high school certificate or diploma, and 11,406 (26.5%) people exclusively held high school qualifications. The median income was $38,200, compared with $41,500 nationally. 3,174 people (7.4%) earned over $100,000 compared to 12.1% nationally. The employment status of those at least 15 was 21,432 (49.8%) full-time, 5,511 (12.8%) part-time, and 2,088 (4.8%) unemployed.[[38]](#cite_note-Census_2023-38)

Rotorua has the highest proportion of Māori of any city in New Zealand.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Government")]

### Local

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Local")]

Main article: [Rotorua Lakes District](/wiki/Rotorua_Lakes_District "Rotorua Lakes District")

In October 2013, [Steve Chadwick](/wiki/Steve_Chadwick "Steve Chadwick") was elected [Mayor of Rotorua](/wiki/Mayor_of_Rotorua "Mayor of Rotorua") and was re-elected in the [2016 mayoral election](/wiki/2016_New_Zealand_local_elections "2016 New Zealand local elections") and the [2019 mayoral election](/wiki/2019_New_Zealand_local_elections "2019 New Zealand local elections"). She previously served as the Member of Parliament for Rotorua between 1999 and 2008, and served as a Cabinet Minister in the [Fifth Labour Government](/wiki/Fifth_Labour_Government_of_New_Zealand "Fifth Labour Government of New Zealand").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In 2022, [Tania Tapsell](/wiki/Tania_Tapsell "Tania Tapsell") was elected as the [Mayor of Rotorua](/wiki/Mayor_of_Rotorua "Mayor of Rotorua"). She is the first woman of [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") descent to hold the role.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### National

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: National")]

Rotorua is covered by the [Rotorua electorate](/wiki/Rotorua_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Rotorua (New Zealand electorate)") for the general roll and the [Waiariki electorate](/wiki/Waiariki_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Waiariki (New Zealand electorate)") for the Māori roll.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Economy")]

In 2024, the [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) of the Rotorua District was $4,857 M, representing 1.2% of New Zealand's total GDP. The industry sector[[Note 1]](#cite_note-44) with the largest contribution to the Rotorua District GDP was agriculture, forestry and fishing, at 10.7%. This is more than twice the 5.0% contribution that this sector makes to the national economy. The next highest contribution to the district GDP was from health care and social assistance, representing 9.1% in the district GDP, versus 6.6% in the national economy. Manufacturing contributed 7.1%, compared with 7.8% in the national economy.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

The industry sector with the highest employment in the district was health care and social assistance, at 12.8% of the filled jobs. The next largest sector was accommodation and food services at 9.8% of the filled jobs. As a major visitor destination, Rotorua District has a higher proportion of jobs in accommodation and food services than the national average of 6.5%.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Tourism (comprising parts of multiple industry sectors) contributed $336.9 million (6.9%) to the district GDP, down from 8% in 2000.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

## Geothermal attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Geothermal attractions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Tikitere_Mud_Pool_n.jpg/250px-Tikitere_Mud_Pool_n.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tikitere_Mud_Pool_n.jpg)

Mud pool, [Tikitere](/wiki/Tikitere "Tikitere") ("Hell's Gate"), Rotorua.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Rotorua_PoW.jpg/250px-Rotorua_PoW.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rotorua_PoW.jpg)

The Prince of Wales Feathers thermal spring erupting

Known as a spa town and major tourist resort since the 1800s, many of Rotorua's buildings reflect this history. [Government Gardens](/wiki/Government_Gardens "Government Gardens"), close to the lake-shore at the eastern edge of the town, include the [Rotorua Museum of Art and History](/wiki/Rotorua_Museum "Rotorua Museum") housed in the large Tudor-style bath house building, and the [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") style [Blue Baths](/wiki/Blue_Baths "Blue Baths"), noted for its embrace of mixed sex bathing in the 1930s. As of October 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit), both buildings are closed because of earthquake strength concerns.[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49)

There are many geothermal attractions in the Rotorua area, including:[[49]](#cite_note-50)

* Kuirau Park, in the central city[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-NZ_Herald_10415083-52)
* [Government Gardens](/wiki/Government_Gardens "Government Gardens"), including Sulphur Point[[52]](#cite_note-NZHPT_7015-53)
* Te Puia – a visitor attraction located in the [Whakarewarewa](/wiki/Whakarewarewa "Whakarewarewa") volcanic valley, and home of the [Pōhutu Geyser](/wiki/P%C5%8Dhutu_Geyser "Pōhutu Geyser")
* [Tikitere](/wiki/Tikitere "Tikitere") – Hell's Gate Geothermal Park & Mud Spa[[53]](#cite_note-54)
* [Te Wairoa](/wiki/Te_Wairoa_(village) "Te Wairoa (village)") ("The Buried Village") — so named after it was buried by the 1886 [Mount Tarawera](/wiki/Mount_Tarawera "Mount Tarawera") eruption
* [Lake Rotomahana](/wiki/Lake_Rotomahana "Lake Rotomahana")
* [Waimangu Volcanic Valley](/wiki/Waimangu_Volcanic_Rift_Valley "Waimangu Volcanic Rift Valley")
* Waikite Valley Thermal Pools[[54]](#cite_note-55)

The especially pungent smell in the central-east 'Te Ngae' area is due to the dense sulphur deposits located next to the southern boundary of the Government Gardens, in the area known as 'Sulphur Point'.

## Other attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Other attractions")]

### Te Puia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Te Puia")]

Te Puia is a major visitor attraction and Māori cultural centre in Rotorua. It encompasses an area of 70 ha (170 acres) within the [Whakarewarewa](/wiki/Whakarewarewa "Whakarewarewa") valley at the southern end of the city, and includes the [Pōhutu Geyser](/wiki/P%C5%8Dhutu_Geyser "Pōhutu Geyser"), mud pools, hot springs, and silica formations. In addition to the geothermal features, Te Puia includes the [New Zealand Māori Arts and Crafts Institute](/wiki/New_Zealand_M%C4%81ori_Arts_and_Crafts_Institute "New Zealand Māori Arts and Crafts Institute") and national schools of wood carving, weaving, stone and bone carving. There is a carved meeting house, *Te Aronui a Rua*, and other facilities for visitors. In 2025, Te Puia was the supreme winner at the New Zealand Tourism Awards.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

### Lakeland Queen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Lakeland Queen")]

Main article: [Lakeland Queen](/wiki/Lakeland_Queen "Lakeland Queen")

*Lakeland Queen* is a [sternwheeler](/wiki/Paddle_steamer#Sternwheel "Paddle steamer") passenger vessel that operates cruises on Lake Rotorua. She was built in Rotorua in 1986 by brothers Ian and Doug Stewart of the company Lakeland Steel Products, in the style of the historic [Mississippi paddle steamers](/wiki/Steamboats_of_the_Mississippi "Steamboats of the Mississippi").[[56]](#cite_note-57) *Lakeland Queen* was launched on 26 October 1986,[[57]](#cite_note-58) and is the only sternwheeler passenger vessel in New Zealand.[[58]](#cite_note-:03-59) *Lakeland Queen* is used for breakfast, lunch and dinner cruises and sightseeing. Māori cultural entertainment has been provided on some cruises.[[59]](#cite_note-60) The cruises often have live music, with Blues Cruises a regular feature.[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)

### Wingspan National Bird of Prey Centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Wingspan National Bird of Prey Centre")]

Main article: [Wingspan National Bird of Prey Centre](/wiki/Wingspan_National_Bird_of_Prey_Centre "Wingspan National Bird of Prey Centre")

Wingspan National Bird of Prey Centre is a captive breeding facility and visitor centre located in the [Ngongotahā Valley](/wiki/Ngongotah%C4%81_Valley "Ngongotahā Valley"). Wingspan undertakes conservation, education and research activities related to [birds of prey](/wiki/Bird_of_prey "Bird of prey") found in New Zealand, and provides demonstrations of [falconry](/wiki/Falconry "Falconry").[[64]](#cite_note-65)

## Sport and outdoor activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Sport and outdoor activities")]

Another of Rotorua's attractions is [mountain biking](/wiki/Mountain_biking "Mountain biking"). Rotorua was listed as one of the Top 6 mountain biking destinations globally by the [International Mountain Biking Association](/wiki/International_Mountain_Biking_Association "International Mountain Biking Association") in 2015, when they awarded Rotorua the gold level ride centre status.[[65]](#cite_note-66) The [Whakarewarewa](/wiki/Whakarewarewa "Whakarewarewa") forest includes over 150 km of mountain bike trails[[66]](#cite_note-67) and in August 2006 was a host of the [UCI Mountain Bike and Trials World Championships](/wiki/UCI_Mountain_Bike_and_Trials_World_Championships "UCI Mountain Bike and Trials World Championships").[[67]](#cite_note-68) Each year, from 2015–present, Rotorua has hosted a round of the [Crankworx World Tour](/wiki/Crankworx "Crankworx").[[68]](#cite_note-69)

The Rotorua Pistol club is among the largest in the Southern Hemisphere and hosted the 2013 Australasian IPSC Handgun Championship.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

The [Kaituna River](/wiki/Kaituna_River "Kaituna River"), 15 minutes drive northeast of the CBD, provides class 5 whitewater kayaking and rafting through a spectacular tree lined gorge.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Road")]

Rotorua is served by [state highways](/wiki/New_Zealand_state_highway_network "New Zealand state highway network") [5](/wiki/State_Highway_5_(New_Zealand) "State Highway 5 (New Zealand)"), [30](/wiki/New_Zealand_State_Highway_30 "New Zealand State Highway 30"), and [30A](/wiki/New_Zealand_State_Highway_30A "New Zealand State Highway 30A"), and the Thermal Explorer Highway touring route, with [state highways 33](/wiki/New_Zealand_State_Highway_33 "New Zealand State Highway 33") and [36](/wiki/New_Zealand_State_Highway_36 "New Zealand State Highway 36") terminating on the outskirts of the city.

State Highway 5, running concurrently with the Thermal Explorer Highway, is the main north–south route through Rotorua, bypassing the city centre to the west. North of the city at Ngongotahā, State Highway 36 splits off to provide a route to [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga") via Pyes Pa, while State Highway 5 turns westward, connecting to [State Highway 1](/wiki/State_Highway_1_(New_Zealand) "State Highway 1 (New Zealand)") at [Tīrau](/wiki/T%C4%ABrau "Tīrau") and providing the main route into Rotorua from [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_New_Zealand "Hamilton, New Zealand") and [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"). To the south, State Highway 5 provides the main route from [Taupō](/wiki/Taup%C5%8D "Taupō"), [Hawke's Bay](/wiki/Hawke%27s_Bay_region "Hawke's Bay region"), [Manawatū](/wiki/Manawat%C5%AB-Whanganui "Manawatū-Whanganui"), and [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington").

State Highway 30 runs southwest to northeast through the city. It enters the city in the southwest (running concurrently with SH 5), before crossing the southern suburbs to the shore of Lake Rotorua east of the city centre. It then runs through the suburb of Te Ngae, before splitting off SH 33 to continue eastwards.

State Highway 30A runs northwest to southeast, connecting State Highways 5 and 30 with each other via the city centre.

### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Bus")]

Rotorua has a local bus service,[[71]](#cite_note-72) with 11 routes under the Baybus brand, serving the urban area, mostly at half-hourly intervals,[[72]](#cite_note-73) operated by [Reesby Buses](/wiki/Brian_Souter#InMotion_Group "Brian Souter").[[73]](#cite_note-74) [Bike racks](/wiki/Bicycle_carrier#New_Zealand "Bicycle carrier") were introduced in 2017[[74]](#cite_note-75) and [Bee Cards](/wiki/Bee_Card_(New_Zealand) "Bee Card (New Zealand)") for fares on 27 July 2020.[[75]](#cite_note-76) Trial commuter services between Rotorua and Tauranga are running in each direction until the end of 2021.[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78) The city is also served by [InterCity](/wiki/InterCity_(New_Zealand) "InterCity (New Zealand)")[[78]](#cite_note-79) and services to local tourist sites.[[79]](#cite_note-80)

#### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: History")]

Edwin Robertson, who died aged 74 in 1931,[[80]](#cite_note-81) started with [pack horses](/wiki/Packhorse "Packhorse") in 1869 and ran coaches from 1873.[[81]](#cite_note-:1-82) In 1902, the Tauranga route was sold and became Robertson & Co,[[82]](#cite_note-83) then, about 1903, Rotorua Motor Coaching Co. Ltd.[[83]](#cite_note-:2-84) In 1904, Hot Lakes Transport, which ran trips to Taupō,[[84]](#cite_note-85) [Waiotapu](/wiki/Waiotapu "Waiotapu") and all the lakes,[[85]](#cite_note-86) and Rotorua Motor Coaching added motor cars to their fleets of coaches.[[86]](#cite_note-87) In 1905 E. Robertson & Co moved from [Ohinemutu](/wiki/Ohinemutu "Ohinemutu") to the new town, close to the new railway station,[[81]](#cite_note-:1-82) which remained the main stop until InterCity moved to their stop from Hinemoa / Fenton Street[[87]](#cite_note-88) to the Tourism Office in 1995.[[88]](#cite_note-89) The last coach ran in 1919.[[89]](#cite_note-:0-90) When Hot Lakes Transport's assets were sold in 1920, they had 10 coaches and 3 motor cars.[[90]](#cite_note-91) In September 1920, Rotorua Motor Transport Co. was formed and took over Hot Lakes Transport Co. and Rotorua Motor Coaching Co,[[91]](#cite_note-92) continuing with similar services.[[92]](#cite_note-93) In 1926, a consortium of local operators formed Rotorua Bus Co.[[93]](#cite_note-94) During 1922, Kusab's transport company became K Motors,[[89]](#cite_note-:0-90) which was taken over by the railways in 1938.[[94]](#cite_note-95) Rotorua Motor Transport and Rotorua Bus Co followed in 1940[[95]](#cite_note-96) and all became part of [New Zealand Railways Road Services](/wiki/New_Zealand_Railways_Road_Services "New Zealand Railways Road Services").[[83]](#cite_note-:2-84)

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Air")]

[Rotorua Regional Airport](/wiki/Rotorua_Regional_Airport "Rotorua Regional Airport") is located 9 km (6 mi) northeast of the city centre, off State Highway 30. [Air New Zealand](/wiki/Air_New_Zealand "Air New Zealand") provides daily [turbo-prop](/wiki/Turbo-prop "Turbo-prop") flights between Rotorua and [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland_Airport "Auckland Airport"), [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington_International_Airport "Wellington International Airport"), and [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch_International_Airport "Christchurch International Airport") airports. Previously [Qantas](/wiki/Qantas "Qantas") also operated Boeing 737 aircraft from Christchurch, but upon their departure from domestic flights in New Zealand this was discontinued.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Scenic and chartered flights in both helicopters and float planes are operated by Volcanic Air, who are based on Rotorua's lake front.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From 2009 to 2015 there was also an international link, with direct Sydney to Rotorua flights.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Rail")]

Rotorua is connected to the rail network by the [Rotorua Branch](/wiki/Rotorua_Branch "Rotorua Branch") line from [Putāruru](/wiki/Put%C4%81ruru "Putāruru"). Until 8 October 2001, passenger trains ran from Auckland to Rotorua via Hamilton daily using Silver Fern railcars, terminating north of the town centre at Koutu (the original station on Amohau Street was closed and relocated to Koutu on 18 August 1989). However, owing to poor advertising of the service and the location of the station being a 15-minute walk from the town centre in an industrial area, passenger services stopped in October 2001. Freight services on the line declined over the decades until the nightly freight service stopped in June 2000, largely due to a continual move of freight and passengers onto road transport using ever-improving highways in the region. The line is currently disused.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of schools in the Bay of Plenty region § Rotorua District](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_the_Bay_of_Plenty_region#Rotorua_District "List of schools in the Bay of Plenty region")

### Tertiary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Tertiary")]

Rotorua is home to the central campus of [Toi Ohomai Institute of Technology](/wiki/Toi_Ohomai_Institute_of_Technology "Toi Ohomai Institute of Technology"), which provides a range of certificates, diplomas and a limited number of degree-level programmes. The largest programmes on offer are Māori language, [nursing](/wiki/Nursing "Nursing"), [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry"), business, [computing](/wiki/Computing "Computing"), tourism and [hospitality](/wiki/Hospitality "Hospitality"). As of June 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit), Toi Ohomai Institute of Technology is a business division of [Te Pūkenga](/wiki/Te_P%C5%ABkenga "Te Pūkenga") – New Zealand Institute of Skills and Technology.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

### Secondary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Secondary")]

Rotorua has five secondary schools:

* [John Paul College](/wiki/John_Paul_College,_Rotorua "John Paul College, Rotorua"), a co-educational, [state-integrated](/wiki/State-integrated_school "State-integrated school") Catholic school for Years 7–13 with about 1200 students[[98]](#cite_note-99)
* [Rotorua Boys' High School](/wiki/Rotorua_Boys%27_High_School "Rotorua Boys' High School"), a state boys school for Years 9–13 with about 1000 students[[99]](#cite_note-100)
* [Rotorua Girls' High School](/wiki/Rotorua_Girls%27_High_School "Rotorua Girls' High School"), a state girls school for Years 9–13 with about 600 students[[100]](#cite_note-101)
* [Rotorua Lakes High School](/wiki/Rotorua_Lakes_High_School "Rotorua Lakes High School") serving the eastern suburbs. A co-educational state school for Years 9–13 with about 700 students[[101]](#cite_note-102)
* [Western Heights High School](/wiki/Western_Heights_High_School "Western Heights High School") serving the western suburbs. A co-educational state school for Years 9–13 with about 1200 students[[102]](#cite_note-103)

Students can also attend Te Rangihakahaka Centre for Science and Techbnology, a Māori- and English-medium special character school from years 1–10.[[103]](#cite_note-104) It currently has around 100 students.[[104]](#cite_note-105) Students here need to attend a mainstream secondary school or [Kura Kaupapa Māori](/wiki/Kura_Kaupapa_M%C4%81ori "Kura Kaupapa Māori") for their senior secondary schooling.
